check: Conversion actions map to real business value, not just page views
description: Each conversion action tracked in Google Ads represents a genuine business outcome (purchase, qualified lead, signup) with an accurate value, not a proxy like 'page view' that doesn't reflect actual value
why_it_matters: Smart Bidding optimizes toward whatever is marked as a conversion — optimizing toward a weak proxy metric actively steers spend toward the wrong outcomes

check: Enhanced Conversions or server-side tagging implemented for conversion accuracy
description: First-party conversion data (hashed email/phone) is passed via Enhanced Conversions or a server-side tagging setup, not relying solely on browser-side pixel firing
why_it_matters: Browser-side-only tracking undercounts conversions due to ad blockers, ITP/cookie restrictions, and consent-mode signal loss — this is the single highest-leverage tracking-accuracy fix available on most accounts today

check: Conversion values reflect real revenue/LTV, not a flat placeholder value
description: Where possible, conversion value passed to Google Ads reflects actual order value or an LTV-informed estimate, not a static placeholder value applied to every conversion
why_it_matters: Value-based Smart Bidding strategies (Target ROAS, Maximize Conversion Value) are only as good as the value data they optimize against — flat placeholder values make ROAS bidding meaningless

check: Campaigns organized around a clear business logic (product line, funnel stage, or geography), not ad-hoc
description: Campaign structure reflects a deliberate organizing principle that maps budget/bidding decisions to real business priorities, not campaigns created ad hoc over time with no consistent logic
why_it_matters: A tangled, ad-hoc account structure makes budget allocation and performance analysis genuinely difficult — this compounds over time and is expensive to untangle later, worth getting right from the start

check: No excessive campaign/ad group fragmentation limiting Smart Bidding's learning data
description: Campaigns/ad groups aren't split so finely that each one gets too little conversion volume for Smart Bidding to exit the learning phase effectively
why_it_matters: Modern Smart Bidding needs sufficient conversion volume per campaign to optimize well — over-segmentation (a legacy keyword-match-type-driven structure habit) starves each campaign of the data it needs

check: Broad match paired with Smart Bidding and strong negative keyword hygiene, not broad match alone
description: If using broad match (Google's current recommended default for Smart Bidding campaigns), it's paired with automated bidding toward a real conversion value and active negative keyword management
why_it_matters: Broad match without Smart Bidding or without negative keyword discipline can waste spend on irrelevant queries — the current best-practice pairing (broad match + Smart Bidding + negatives) is different from older exact-match-heavy conventions

check: Search terms report reviewed regularly, negative keywords added proactively
description: The search terms report is reviewed on a recurring cadence (not just when performance drops) to catch irrelevant query matches and add negatives before they accumulate wasted spend
why_it_matters: Waiting to review search terms only after noticing a budget/performance problem means wasted spend has already accumulated — proactive review catches it earlier

check: Landing page experience, ad relevance, and expected CTR all reviewed, not just bid levels
description: When diagnosing high CPCs or low impression share, Quality Score's three components are checked individually rather than assuming the fix is always 'bid higher'
why_it_matters: A low Quality Score driven by poor landing page experience won't be fixed by bidding higher — misdiagnosing which QS component is weak wastes budget chasing the wrong fix

check: PMax search-term insights and asset group performance reviewed regularly, not treated as a black box
description: PMax campaigns are actively monitored via search-term insights (where available) and per-asset-group performance, not launched and left unattended on the assumption automation handles everything
why_it_matters: PMax's automation reduces manual control but doesn't eliminate the need for oversight — brand exclusions, asset quality, and search-term relevance still need active management (`ADS010`, `ADS011`)

check: Brand exclusions configured to prevent PMax from bidding on the advertiser's own branded searches at a wasteful rate
description: Where a separate branded search campaign already exists, PMax is configured (via brand exclusion lists) to avoid competing with it for branded query traffic at inflated cost
why_it_matters: Without exclusions, PMax can cannibalize cheap, high-intent branded traffic that a dedicated branded campaign would capture more efficiently, inflating blended CPA

check: AI Brief / text customization used to guide PMax messaging, not left fully automated with generic assets
description: Where the AI Brief / asset customization tools are available, they're used to steer PMax's generated messaging toward the actual value proposition rather than leaving fully generic default assets to be auto-combined
why_it_matters: Fully generic PMax assets with no brand-specific input produce fully generic ad combinations — the tools to guide this exist and are underused relative to their impact

check: Bidding strategy matches actual business goal (ROAS target reflects real margin, CPA target reflects real economics)
description: Target ROAS/CPA values entered into Smart Bidding reflect the account's actual unit economics (margin, LTV), not an arbitrary or aspirational number disconnected from what the business can sustain
why_it_matters: A ROAS/CPA target set too aggressively either starves the campaign of volume (target unreachable) or, set too loosely, wastes spend on unprofitable conversions — this needs to be grounded in real numbers

check: Sufficient learning-phase conversion volume before judging a new bidding strategy's performance
description: A newly launched or recently-changed Smart Bidding strategy is given adequate time and conversion volume (commonly cited as roughly 2-4 weeks / 30+ conversions, though this varies) before being judged as underperforming and changed again
why_it_matters: Repeatedly changing bidding strategy before it exits the learning phase resets the algorithm's learning each time, producing consistently mediocre performance that never reflects the strategy's actual steady-state potential

check: Ad strength rated Good/Excellent, with diverse, non-redundant asset variations
description: Responsive Search/Display ad assets include genuinely varied headlines and descriptions (not near-duplicates of each other) sufficient to earn a Good/Excellent ad strength rating
why_it_matters: Redundant, low-diversity assets limit Google's ability to test and serve the best-performing combination, and correlate with weaker overall ad performance beyond just the ad-strength score itself

check: All relevant ad extensions/assets enabled (sitelinks, callouts, structured snippets, images)
description: Available ad assets (sitelinks, callouts, structured snippets, price, image assets where applicable) are configured, not left at the bare minimum
why_it_matters: Ad assets increase ad real estate on the results page and are a factor in ad rank — leaving them unconfigured is free performance left unclaimed

check: First-party audience data (customer match, site visitors) used to inform Smart Bidding, not ignored
description: Available first-party data (customer lists, remarketing audiences) is uploaded and used as an audience signal for Smart Bidding/PMax, not left unused while the account relies solely on Google's own signals
why_it_matters: First-party audience signals give Smart Bidding a meaningfully stronger starting point than cold automated targeting alone, particularly valuable given increasing third-party signal loss

check: Ad-to-landing-page message match verified for the top-spending campaigns
description: The landing page a top-spending ad points to genuinely matches the ad's specific offer/message, not a generic homepage or a page discussing a different product
why_it_matters: Message mismatch between ad and landing page directly hurts both Quality Score (`ADS008`) and conversion rate — a click that lands somewhere unexpected is a wasted click even if the ad itself performed well

check: Shared negative keyword lists used for account-wide exclusions (competitor brand terms, irrelevant intent)
description: Common exclusions that apply across many/all campaigns (competitor brand names, clearly irrelevant intent terms like 'free' or 'jobs' where not relevant) are maintained as a shared list rather than duplicated per campaign
why_it_matters: Shared lists ensure a new campaign automatically inherits established exclusions rather than requiring the same negative keyword work to be redone (and potentially forgotten) for every new campaign

check: Location targeting matches actual serviceable area, 'Presence' targeting confirmed deliberately vs 'Presence or Interest'
description: Location targeting settings are reviewed explicitly — the difference between targeting people physically present in a location versus people merely interested in it can matter significantly for a local/service-area business
why_it_matters: Getting this setting wrong for a local business can mean paying for clicks from users who were never actually reachable/serviceable, a subtle and easy-to-miss budget leak

check: Ad scheduling reflects when the business can actually respond to leads/traffic, not running 24/7 by default
description: For lead-gen businesses specifically, ad scheduling is reviewed against actual business hours/capacity to respond, rather than left at the default of running continuously
why_it_matters: Spending budget generating leads outside business hours when nobody can respond promptly measurably hurts lead quality/conversion rate for time-sensitive lead-gen businesses
